ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general cuttlefish food product class; no specific entry for dried seasoned cuttlefish snack strips. Closest defensible match used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A0CKP covers processed cephalopod products; used as closest defensible anchor for dried seasoned cuttlefish snack.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT code identified for dried seasoned cuttlefish snack; left null to maintain ontology honesty.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical single serving of cuttlefish snack is approximately 30 g, equivalent to one small retail snack pack or a small handful of chewy strips.

nutrient_source.title: Cuttlefish snack — HPB Singapore Foodo Nutrient Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Values taken directly from HPB Singapore Foodo entry for cuttlefish snack (F03040005). All core macros and micronutrients sourced from this record at confidence 0.95.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — analogous estimate based on dried sweetened seafood snack with high sugar and low fibre profile
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson FS et al., Diabetes Care 2021
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for dried seasoned cuttlefish snack in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI estimated at 65 (medium) based on analogous dried sweetened seafood snack products with comparable carbohydrate content (~49g/100g), high added sugar (~18g/100g), and negligible dietary fibre (0.2g/100g). Glycemic load per 30g serving = (65 × 49.0 × 0.30) / 100 = 9.56, rounded to 9.5. This is a conservative medium-GI estimate; actual GI may be higher given the high sugar and low fibre content.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 923 mg per 100 g — far exceeds the 600 mg/100 g upper threshold for hypertension-sensitive foods
hypertension.guidance: Individuals managing hypertension should avoid cuttlefish snack; a single 30 g serving delivers approximately 277 mg sodium, which represents over 18% of the 1500 mg daily sodium target recommended for hypertensive adults, and regular consumption poses a significant risk of blood pressure elevation.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High added sugar (~18 g/100 g) combined with negligible dietary fibre (0.2 g/100 g) and medium-to-high estimated GI promotes rapid postprandial glucose elevation
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should avoid or strictly limit cuttlefish snack because the high sugar content and near-absent fibre produce rapid glucose spikes; a 30 g serving contributes approximately 5.4 g sugar and 14.7 g total carbohydrate with minimal glycaemic buffering.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Very high sodium (923 mg/100 g) and high added sugar content elevate cardiovascular risk through blood pressure and metabolic pathways
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with or at risk of cardiovascular disease should avoid cuttlefish snack due to its very high sodium content driving hypertensive cardiovascular risk and its high sugar load contributing to dyslipidaemia and metabolic syndrome; the low fat content (0.8 g/100 g) does not offset these concerns.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Cuttlefish snack is a popular processed seafood snack widely consumed in Singapore and across Southeast Asia. It consists of dried, seasoned cuttlefish — a cephalopod mollusc — that has been processed into chewy, flat strips or shreds and coated with a sweet-savoury seasoning blend typically containing sugar, salt, and flavour enhancers. The texture is characteristically chewy and slightly fibrous, with a sweet, salty, and umami-rich flavour profile. Per 100 g, cuttlefish snack provides 353 kcal, 37.5 g protein, 49.0 g carbohydrate (of which approximately 18 g is sugar), 0.8 g fat, and 923 mg sodium. The high protein content reflects the cuttlefish base, while the high carbohydrate and sugar content result from the seasoning process. Dietary fibre is negligible at 0.2 g/100 g. Clinically, this snack is unsuitable for individuals managing hypertension due to its very high sodium content (923 mg/100 g), and unsuitable for those with Type 2 Diabetes due to high added sugar and negligible fibre causing rapid postprandial glucose spikes. The high sodium also poses significant cardiovascular risk. It is commonly sold in small retail packs as a snack food and is found in convenience stores, supermarkets, and hawker stalls across Singapore and Malaysia. It is not suitable for vegetarians or vegans and contains mollusc allergens.
common_user_queries: How much sodium is in cuttlefish snack?
Is cuttlefish snack high in protein?
Is dried cuttlefish snack suitable for hypertension?

confidence_reason: Core macronutrient and micronutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (F03040005) at confidence 0.95. Source is an approved primary regional source for Singapore foods. GI is an analogous estimate (medium confidence component) but all other values are exact matches from the approved source.
notes: saturated_fat_g, monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, cholesterol_mg, potassium_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid profile, cholesterol, micronutrient panel, and moisture data for this processed cuttlefish snack product are not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry F03040005 as of 2026. sugar_g value of 18.0 g/100 g is estimated based on the total carbohydrate content (49.0 g/100 g) and the known formulation of sweetened dried cuttlefish snacks; actual sugar content may vary by brand and formulation — consumers should check product packaging. GI of 65 is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database framework applied to dried sweetened seafood snacks with comparable macronutrient profiles; no direct GI measurement for this specific product was found in the approved GI database. Halal status is assumed based on the cuttlefish base ingredient being permissible under most halal standards, but consumers should verify product-level halal certification. Gluten-free status is uncertain as some seasoning blends may contain wheat-derived ingredients.
